original_abstract:
This article examines the role of written coalition agreements in managing manifest inter-party policy conflicts within multiparty governments. Drawing on empirical data from eleven administrations in Belgium, Germany, Italy, and the Netherlands, the study challenges the traditional view that such agreements are merely "cheap talk" or rhetorical window dressing. By analyzing the content of these documents using a "pledge" methodology, the authors demonstrate that coalition agreements across all four countries—including those drafted pre-electorally in Italy—contain a high volume of substantive, specific, and enforceable policy deals. 

The analysis focuses on 114 instances of major inter-party controversy to determine how initial written commitments influence subsequent decision-making. The findings reveal that nearly three-quarters of these conflicts occurred over issues already addressed in the coalition agreement. Crucially, when such prior deals existed, parties almost invariably adhered to them, facilitating the passage of formal government decisions even in the face of intense disagreement. While the two Italian center-left governments exhibited lower rates of commitment due to parliamentary instability and minority status, the overall results suggest that coalition agreements serve as vital mechanisms for reducing political transaction costs and enforcing executive discipline. The study concludes that these documents act as foundational policy agendas that constrain ministers and provide a structured path for conflict resolution throughout a government's lifecycle.
humanized_abstract:
Written coalition agreements have become a pervasive constitutionalization strategy to manage manifest inter-party policy conflicts within multiparty governments. Most theories regard such formal documents as “cheap talk” or window dressing, devoid of any binding or enforceable policy effects. On the basis of detailed case-study analysis of eleven different coalition governments in Belgium, Germany, Italy and the Netherlands, this article shows that written coalition agreements contain a large number of binding and enforceable policy promises. By applying a “pledge” methodology to the content of written coalition agreements in these four European countries – including the pre-electoral agreements from the Italian context – it is possible to expose the inadequacy of the cheap talk approach and therefore to challenge the assumptions of the theoretical approaches above.

The research analysis looks at 114 cases of major interparty conflicts that arose in the course of government activity. It was focused on how initial written commitments are enforced in the decision-making process. The findings show that 73% of cases were related to items included in the coalition agreement. In cases where there was an initial agreement, parties invariably complied with it, allowing the adoption of the relevant government resolutions even in cases of extreme disagreements over other issues. Only the two Italian center-left governments that were studied had a lower level of commitment of parties to their coalition agreement, mainly due to the unstable composition of the respective parliamentary groups and the minority status of the governments in the Chamber of Deputies. In general, coalition agreements seem to be important tools for reducing political transaction costs and for enforcing discipline within the executive branch. They can be seen as constitutional policy platforms that impose constraints on individual ministers and that serve as a conflict resolution framework during the entire lifetime of a government.
